questions.text: Identify the correct statement from the following: What is meant by the construct 'limited liability' in the context of a limited liability form of legal entity?

options.label: a
options.text: The liability of the company is limited to its resources.

options.label: b
options.text: The liability of any owner is limited to his/her share of the business at any point in

options.label: c
options.text: The liability of any owner is limited to his/her proportional shareholding in the

options.label: d
options.text: The liability of a company is limited to the market value of its assets.

questions.explanation: NPTEL Online Certification Courses Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur The liability of any owner is limited to his/her share of the business at any point in time. His/her/ their personal assets are not at risk for the recovery of any company debts. Imagine a person, 'X,' who has invested Rs. 1.00 lakh in a business and received 10,000 shares (assuming a face value or par value of Rs. 10/- for each share), constituting 20% of the shareholding. Suppose the company borrows Rs. 100 lakh from a bank and defaults. Let's assume the bank obtains a court decree to recover the money from the company. The court is entitled to receive proceeds by selling the company's assets but cannot touch the personal assets of 'X' or any other shareholders. Consequently, in the liquidation process, 'X' may have to forfeit their shares without affecting personal assets. Option 'c' in the question suggests that liability is PROPORTIONAL to shareholding, implying that 'X' must pay Rs. 10 lakh to the bank. However, option 'b' accurately states that liability is limited to the SHARES one owns (and not the shareholding percentage). Of course, the value of the shares may change over time.

questions.text: A company sells pens. Following information pertains to its operation in a particular year (All data are in Rupees except when otherwise mentioned): Per unit selling price of pen: ₹ 10 Number of pens sold during the year: 100 (number) Closing Stock: ₹ 100 Depreciation: ₹ 100 Opening Stock: ₹ 50 Amortization: ₹100 Purchase of goods: ₹ 800 What is the gross profit margin during this period?
questions.explanation: Gross profit = Sales minus cost of goods sold (CoGS). CoGS = Opening stock + Purchase during the period - closing stock. Sales = number of pen sold multiplied by the selling price of each pen = 100*10 = 1,000. Depreciation and amortization are not part of the CoGS. CoGS = 50+800-100 = 750. GP = 10*100 - 750 = 250 GP Margin = 250/1,000*100 = 25% ______________________________________________________________________

questions.text: Following data are related to a company for the financial year 2016-2017. (All figures are in Rupees except when specifically mentioned otherwise) Sales: 1,000 Gross profit: 800 Interest payment: 50 Depreciation: 50 Salary: 250 Rent: 50 Other operating expenses: 200 Dividend: 100 What is the operating profit margin for the company during 2016-17?
questions.explanation: Operating profit = Gross profit - operating expenses. Operating expenses include all expenses including salary, rent, depreciation & amortization but excluding interest. Dividend is not an expense. The dividend is paid to owners out of net profit. Operating profit = 800 - (50+250+50+200) = 800 - 550 = 250 OP margin = 250/1000*100 = 25% ______________________________________________________________________

questions.text: Following data are related to a company for the financial year 2021-2022. (all figures are in Rupees except when specifically mentioned otherwise) Gross profit: 800 Interest payment: 50 Depreciation: 50 Other operating expenses: 400 Dividend: 100 Assume that the company doesn't pay any income tax, what is the retained profit?
questions.explanation: Retained profit = operating profit - interest - income tax - dividend. In this case, the company does not pay any income tax. Operating profit = Gross profit - operating expenses. Retained profit = 800 (Gross profit) - [(400 + 50) (operating expense) - 50 (interest) - 100 (dividend)] = 200 ______________________________________________________________________

questions.text: Following data are related to a company for the financial year 2021-2022. (all figures are in Rupees except when specifically mentioned otherwise) Gross profit: 600 Amortization: 10 Interest payment: 50 Depreciation: 40 Other operating expenses: 200 Dividend: 100 If the income tax is 30% what is the EBITDA?
questions.explanation: Retained earnings is equal to net profit minus dividend. Net profit is equal to profit before tax minus income tax. Profit before tax is equal to operating profit minus interest payment. Operating profit = Gross profit - Operating expenses. EBITDA = Gross profit - Operating expenses other than depreciation and amortization. Income tax and dividend are not to be deducted = 600 - 200 = 400 _______________________________________________________________

questions.text: The following data are related to a company for the financial year 2021-2022. (All figures are in Rupees except when specifically mentioned otherwise) Gross profit: 950 Interest payment: 50 Depreciation: 50 Other operating expenses: 250 Dividend: 100 Reserves and surplus as of 31-3-2021: 900 The company pays income tax @30% What is the reserves and surplus of the company as of 31-3-2022?
questions.explanation: Reserves & Surplus is equal to the previous year balance plus the retained earnings. Retained earnings is equal to net profit minus dividend. Net profit is equal to profit before tax minus income tax. Profit before tax is equal to operating profit minus interest payment. Operating profit = 950 - (50+50+250) = 600. Income tax = 600*.3 = 180. Net profit = 600 - 180 = 420. Transferred to Reserves & Surplus = 420 - 100 = 320. Previous reserves = 900. Present reserves = 900 + 320 = 1,220 ************END******* NPTEL Online Certification Courses Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur

questions.text: A business angel invests 100 million in a startup for a pre-money valuation of 400 million. The angel has 2X liquidity and participatory preference. The company later has a liquidity event (is sold) for 800 million. How much money the founders will receive?

options.label: a
options.text: 600 million

options.label: b
options.text: 480 million

options.label: c
options.text: 640 million

options.label: d
options.text: 360 million

questions.explanation: The liquidity clause tells that the investor should get their money back at the first instance before any other calculation. The X in the liquidation clause indicates how many times of the investment the investors are to receive. A 2X liquidation means that two times the money invested is to be paid to investors before any other calculation. Participatory means the investors participate in the equity and their holding percentage is estimated based on the pre-money valuation and the amount of fund brought in by them. In the present sum, the angel has 2X liquidity. So, they are given 2X100 = ₹200 million before any other estimation. The remaining sales proceed is: 800 - 200 = 600 million. Now, the investors have a participation in the equity. What is their percentage holding in the equity? Pre-money value = 400. They invests 100, so the post-money valuation is 400+100 = 500. Their holding percentage = (100/500) *100 = 20%. Therefore, they should now receive 20% of the remaining amount or 20% of 600 = 120 million. Therefore, total money received by investors = 200 + 120 = 320, leaving behind ₹480 million (=800-320) for the founders.

questions.text: A company raises 500 million rupees at a post-money valuation of 1000 million rupees in the form of simple participating preference shares with no liquidation preference. A few years later the company realizes a liquidity event and is acquired for 5000 million rupees. How much money the founders would receive?

options.text: 4000 million rupees

options.label: c
options.text: 2500 million rupees

options.text: 1500 million rupees

questions.explanation: Participating preference shares eventually provide proportional holding in the equity. For the ₹ 500 million investment, the investors would receive [500/1000] = 50% ownership, while the founders would receive the remaining 50%. Since there is no liquidation preference, the founders would receive 50% of the liquidation proceeds, i.e. 0.50X5000 = ₹ 2500 million. In case: If they had 1X liquidation preference and no participation, they would only receive back their investment, i.e. ₹ 500 million and the founders would receive the remaining ₹ 4500 million. If the investors had 2X liquidation preference, they would receive 2 times their investment,i.e. 2X500 = ₹ 1000 million and the founders would receive ₹ 4000 million. If the investor had 1x liquidation preference and participation (meaning they hold equity of 50%), they would first receive the 1 multiple of their investment, i.e. 500 million and then receive 50% of the rest 4000, i.e. ₹ 2000 million taking the total to ₹ 2500 million and the founders would receive the remaining ₹ 2500 million. questions.text: Question: Read the following statements regarding estimating discounted net present value and answer the question following them. a) It is assumed that all cash flows other than the initial investment occur at the end of respective periods. b) All cash flows generated by an investment project are immediately reinvested at a rate of return equal to the IRR of the project. c) All cash flows generated by an investment project are immediately reinvested at a rate of return equal to the discount rate. d) All cash flows other than the initial investment occur at the beginning of respective periods.

options.label: a
options.text: A & C

options.label: b
options.text: A & D

options.label: c
options.text: B & D

options.label: d
options.text: C & D

questions.explanation: Discounted NPV assumes that all cash flows after the initial investment occur at the end of the respective period. It also assumes that cash flows generated by the project are reinvested at the discount rate. This is one reason NPV is considered more realistic than IRR in many capital budgeting decisions. Why the other options are wrong: b. A is correct, but D is wrong because cash flows are assumed to occur at the end, not beginning, of periods. c. B is an IRR-related reinvestment assumption, not the NPV assumption; D is also wrong. d. C is correct, but D is wrong.

questions.text: Question: Which of the following statements is incorrect? Options:

options.label: b

options.label: c
options.text: The break-even point estimation takes into account the time value of money.

options.label: d
options.text: The payback period method does not take into account the time value of money.

questions.explanation: Break-even analysis does not consider the time value of money. It simply tells how many units or how much sales value is needed to cover fixed and variable costs. It is not a discounted cash flow method. Why the other options are wrong: a. This is true because different methods measure different things. NPV checks value creation using discounted cash flows, while break-even checks cost-volume-profit relationship. b. This is true. Hurdle rate is the minimum acceptable return. d. This is true. Simple payback period ignores the time value of money. c. This is incorrect, so it is the answer.

questions.text: Question: A business contract requires Rs700 initial investment at year zero. It is likely to generate an annual net cash flow of Rs200 for five years beginning from year one and return no salvage value at the end of the business horizon. Use rate of return at 10%. The present value multiplication factor for 10% discount rate for 5 years is 3.79. Estimate the net present value rounded off to full rupee. Options:

options.label: a
options.text: Rs58

options.label: b
options.text: Rs258

options.label: c
options.text: Rs358

options.label: d
options.text: Rs158

questions.explanation: NPV = Present value of future cash inflows - Initial investment. Annual cash flow = Rs200 PV factor = 3.79 Present value of inflows = 200 x 3.79 = Rs758 Initial investment = Rs700 NPV = 758 - 700 = Rs58 Since the NPV is positive, the project creates value above the discount rate. Why the other options are wrong: b. Rs258 overstates the NPV by adding too much value. c. Rs358 is also an overestimate. d. Rs158 is a calculation error. a. Rs58 is obtained by correctly discounting the five-year cash flows and subtracting initial investment.

questions.text: Question: What is hurdle rate? Options:

questions.explanation: Hurdle rate is the minimum acceptable return for a project. It is usually based on the company's weighted average cost of capital plus a risk premium depending on the project. Why the other options are wrong: a. IRR is a project's internal return, but hurdle rate is the minimum required return used to judge that IRR or NPV. b. This confuses hurdle rate with discounted payback logic. c. It says "return on equity," which is too narrow. Hurdle rate applies to the whole project. d. This is the correct definition.
